speaker: interviewer
text: So you heard the shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard the shot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I-- he was a nice man. He used to tell me about the news. He would somehow-- he had us-- somebody would give him an old newspaper, a Ukrainian little newspaper. And when he finished it, he gave it-- he used to give it to me, the

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe weeks old. But, but, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, it wasn't-- but you-- and the news were not-- you know, it was like-- but you could read between lines, figure things out. Like, for instance, when the Germans were retreating, they would say-- I remember that word. It was [SPEAKING UKRAINIAN]. It was in Ukrainian. It means 'a planned retreat.' I was constantly-- when it was said-- I'd hear of 'planned retreats.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew that the war may be going a different direction. But who knows how long it would take or anything like that? But I just-- you couldn't think of long term. You just wondered from day to day whether you're going to make it. Are you going to survive? Or somebody, they'll get you? Because they were constantly hunting for, for Jews. How did you stay-- well, I don't know if you were healthy. But during the winter, were you always sick? What, what was your physical condition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a time-- I've missed-- I missed part of that. But there was a time [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you know that place that I told you we escaped [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from, where they-- you know, the last eight people were there. At one time, they came-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was a-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I have to think. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Before that happened, there was a time where a couple people-- a policeman came. And he said he needs several people for another place to work. And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I was among five or six people. I was sick at that time. I was so sick. And it's the flu or what, I don't remember. But I remember I was shaking. And this farmer who took us to this other place felt sorry for me. When I got there, he took me into his house. And they kept me in the house. And the rest of people were in a-- in another place, in a room, two rooms. I don't remember exactly. And that's the time I remember I was so sick. And I didn't-- I had a temperature and all that. And I don't know what they did, but they gave me something. They tried to take care of me while I was there. And as soon I got better, I joined the rest of the group. And there was also some kind of a smaller farm. And, and these few people, I think one of them was some kind of a mechanic, and-- or two of them. So they were working there. And I also remember, there was one woman. She was a attractive woman. And every night, police, police-- a couple of these policemen would come in. And, and, and, and, and, and they'd take her with them. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: She was another worker?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was one of the, you know, survivors. I mean, she was another Jewish person. I don't know what she was doing. I guess there was-- she was working. I don't know what she was doing, what kind of work she did. I stayed mostly inside. And these people were doing some kind of farm work. But every night that happened with this woman. And I guess, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as I understand it, they were having sex with her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and, you know, something blanked out then. And I don't remember exactly how I left that place to-- I took off on my own before-- I knew that something is going to happen, that they're going to kill. So I don't remember exactly what happened with the rest of them. But this happened to be at a different time. I, I, I-- I'm going way back. But going back to that farmer that I used to work-- in that village I used to work. I met up with another man after I started hiding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was really-- I didn't go too far. The farmer next door to where I worked, I was hiding there in his barn.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already 1944. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What season?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, I think also late in the fall because-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] yeah, that was in the fall of '44. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I met up with this man. And we stayed together. We were-- we had a-- we were hiding in the same place. And at night, we would go out and either get some food. And there was a time we used to-- he was-- he lived in that area. He was-- he was familiar with most of these farmers. And he knew their names, everything. I did too, after a while. We would go into a-- and steal-- this one place, we would, would get chickens. We would steal chickens at night. He had this old lady-- it was a poor lady. And she would cook the chicken. And she would get half of us-- half of it what she would give to us. So for a while, we had-- we had it made. But that was amazing how we used to do that. We got a-- it's like we developed the skill to get a chicken, to kill-- at night when they're sleeping. And, and if you do it quietly, you would grab the chicken by the neck and, and squeeze it so hard, the chicken didn't make much sound. Otherwise, it would wake up all the chicken. And they make a lot of noise. So we were getting good at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the man. He, he was already-- lost his wife and two children. He's one of the guys that buried his own two children. They were shot with his wife. They were separated. And she was killed. And the kids were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was sort of a good man, but an angry man. He was constantly-- I can always remember that. But we were buddies. I mean, we were friends. And then we teamed up with another man who was also in hiding in that village. And mostly, there were hidden-- I used to provide the food, except for he and I with the chicken. I would go in the houses and, and ask for food. And I would provide it for them because this other, third man never did that. He was just always in hiding. He was scared to death. And I was always willing to do that. And I-- they felt that I could get away with it. That fall was getting-- was getting very hard for us in that village because they knew there were three Jewish people there. Ukrainians found out about it. They were in that village. But they didn't know exactly where they were. So they were constantly looking for us. And the man-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. In Yiddish, 'Fawysz' was his nickname. And they, they were scared of him because he was a rugged individual. He was a very strong man. For some reason, they always thought that he carries a gun with him. And, and they, they were after him more so than anybody else, not that-- so they knew about him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, and things were getting so bad, we were afraid to be where we were. Because we thought, gosh, they-- they're getting closer and closer. So we decided to, to build a hiding place in the open field.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fawysz was one of the men with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And one was named Moishe. He was actually-- Moishe was from my little town, my shtetl. And Fawysz [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was somewhere from a little village. He lived on a farm. He had a farm, and-- right near that village where we, we were at.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided to, to build this hiding area in the field. And we would do it at night. We would go out and, and dig and, and just, you know, and make sure it's camouflaged and everything is right. We finally got to the point where it's just about done. I mean, all it all was is a hole in the ground. How did you dig?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- well, we had a shovel. And we had a basket of-- we filled up that was, like, topsoil. It was black. Everything was black. And we dispersed that, you know, dug up some of the-- and, and dispersed it on the field. It was open field, plowed. This was in the fall, where-- and we, we would make sure that we didn't see any footsteps. There was, like, a border between one farmer and the other. And by that border was, like, grass. So this is how they could tell which land this is. This is where it ends, one farmer's land, and that's where it begins, the other farmer's land. It's maybe about two feet of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So right by that border, we dug the, the hole underneath. Because we could walk on the grass. They wouldn't see footprints right away. And when we-- the hole was finished, we brought some straw in and everything and had it all fixed up. And this basket-- we had a big basket. We filled it up with topsoil. And when we-- the last person got in, pulled that basket down. And, and you couldn't tell. Because one guy-- you know, we tested it. We tried it. One guy was outside watching to see what happens. How is it camouflaged? It was just great.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not a soul knew that we are doing it. Because they couldn't see it, we thought, you know. It was dark. And it's away from houses. Well, we decided that that-- that that night we're going to move from the-- from that area from the barn to the open field. And so that decision was made. And we were just about ready to move. I said, it's not a good idea to go there. I don't want to go. I-- for some reason, I was-- I felt there's-- if something to come in a barn-- I mean, you know, this is an open field. There's no place to go. They could spot you. They could do anything. And, you know, if you want to run, they'll shoot you. It's open. Well, that aggravated these other two guys. I mean, these are older-- they are adults. And I'm the kid. And I said, I don't feel like going. And it's like, they, they-- it's not the right thing to do. I mean, we have to be unanimous on this agreement. Yeah, we're going to do it together. And here, he doesn't want to do it. So they were very angry with me. They-- and they didn't go. Well, the very next day, we found out that somehow, somebody saw us digging that. And, and there's about, I don't know, several of these Ukrainians came, surrounded that area, and they thought we were there. And they had, I think-- we were told they were there with a machine gun or automatic rifle. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they found no one. But they came the day after we were supposed to be there. So it was like I had communications with God, you know. They, they, they had respect for me after that, these two guys, because if we would have gone, we would have been all dead or all killed. So there was another time in my life that-- I mean, it happened all through these years, so many times I could have been dead. And somehow we made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think that every time when you came so close to dying, you never gave up? You talked about feeling like you had nothing to live for. You thought-- you thought your entire family was dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, but every time, you keep moving on. What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime I've wondered about that. I, I-- what did-- I've wondered more about the, the way of dying, fear of dying. If I were hit by a bomb, wouldn't-- that would be OK because-- but just to wait to be killed by all these Ukrainians, whether they're going to torture me and do that-- I've seen what they've done to a-- to a family. I was-- I was in the attic when they did it next door. They, they wouldn't even use bullets. They used bayonets. The whole family, they, they, they killed them. They butchered them. I mean, these were just worse than animals. They just had no regards of human lives. They just-- so I think the fear-- how I'm going to die-- catching me and all this. I don't know what it was, really. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I guess, maybe the will for survival-- that I wasn't even aware of it. It was so great. It was so great. And things happened. And sometime, I think about it. It was almost humanly impossible to survive like I did. I found out later on I was the only kid that survived. I was the only youngster who survived from my little shtetl. There was no one. I mean, these two men were never in my shtetl. Like, this Moishe, I mean, came from my home. But he was never-- he lived somewhere else. So [SIGHS] I, I, I-- sometime, I don't understand that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And right-- well, that winter, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one night, we found out that the Russians came through. The Russians were, you know, going towards west, marching. They came through the village. And I thought, boy, we were liberated. But then they were gone. And the same thing starts. So we don't know. We didn't know what to do. We were in hiding. We were scared to, to come out. And then, like, I don't know, five, six days later, the troops were retreating. So they came through that village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which troops?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the Russian troops, you know, who were going to that direction. Now they were going back, going to a different direction. And when they came through again, I decided, I'm going to run with them. So I had no idea where they were going. But I thought, I'm going to go with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was so much snow. And at that time, I, I had-- I remember, my, my shoes were-- this old lady that used to do the-- cook the chicken for us, this-- Fawysz and I went-- he, he, he knew she had her Sunday shoes. And we took her shoes she used to go to church with. They were very kind of summerish. And this is in wintertime-- anyway. So I was walking, following the Russian troops. They were not motorized. They were a cavalry unit, an infantry, and mostly horses and wagon. There was a time I was on the wagon for a little bit. But then I had to get off because they all were walking. And god, this happened all night long.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came to the city of Lutsk, which I was-- in the city. And actually, there was a battle going on. There was the Styr, which normally is from my little town, goes all the way to the city of Lutsk. And, and, and then Lutsk-- on one side of the Styr were the Russians. The other side were the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the Styr?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Styr was the name of the river. The river, the other side of the river. And I was sick. I, I had this breakout of pimples all over my hands. I couldn't even use my fingers because it was-- and I forgot-- there is a name for that sickness, [NON-ENGLISH], you know, eaten by lice and, and filth and dirt. And this is what came about. And I end up in a hospital, in, in, in Lutsk hospital. And there was also a lot of soldiers in that hospital. And they treat it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how I got into the hospital, to be honest with you. I mean, I just-- but I do remember being there. The first night-- the first night, I was in a room when they brought in a, a woman, a young woman, I guess, a young mother that was drinking wooden alcohol. And she died in that room. And I was with-- all night in the same room. Then the next day, there was a Russian officer came-- who was in that room with me. And I remember there were airplanes bombarding. And at that time, it didn't bother me if they-- if they-- if they drop a bomb, and I get killed. I, I didn't fear that at all. So when we-- you know, thinking about dying, that part wasn't so bad. [SIGHS] I stayed in the hospital for a few days. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then this officer who's with me in the room-- I mean, he's a nice man. And I thought maybe I could stick with him or something. I mean, I always tried to leech onto somebody. I, I, I look for somebody. And he says, you can't be with me. He says, I have to go on the front line. In fact, there's-- two or three soldiers came in. And, and they-- and he, he got dressed. And he was leaving. So I got out of the hospital. And here was like a battle zone. You see wounded soldiers and-- on sleds, on-- in-- and dogs pulling them. You could see it. I mean, you could see-- yeah, I don't know if you're familiar with Katyusha rockets. At that time, the, the Russians, that was like their secret weapon. And I saw these Katyusha rockets firing on the other side. Well, I was the only kid. I mean, it's all soldiers. There's no civilians. You don't see anybody on the-- on, on, on the outside. Well, there's a Russian army trucks going back and forth. And I hitchhiked. It's-- it, it, it was pointing to Rowne, which was further east. And guy stopped the truck and let me get in. And it was just the truck driver and I driving. And I think he, he also needed me for a reason. I think the choke was-- he had to hold onto the choke for him to drive. It was an old truck. And, and it was-- I remember, he gave me a-- it was a piece of wood and-- to hold it on. Because this way, it relieves his-- you know, he could do the driving.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got to Rowne. Rowne was further east. It was only about 60 kilometers, 40, 50 miles. And I end up in Rowne.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's when I ran into one of my cousins who survived, Uncle Meir's son. She was about two or-- about three years older than I was. And was I happy to see him. And he was-- while the war was going on, he wanted to, to go into the army. He wanted to volunteer in the Russian army, so he could take revenge, kill Germans. And I would have liked to do that myself. I wanted that, too. But I was just-- at that time, I think I was about 14 or 15. And I was small. And they just didn't-- but-- he was-- he was taken into the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the same cousin that had survived--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the killings that your father did not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. He's the-- he's the one that survived. He joined some partisans. And he did-- but he-- all he could see was just taking revenge. He wanted to do that. He was such a nice guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moishe was his name. Pressman was his last name. Just a nice guy, good-looking kid. [SIGHS] So he, he joined the army. And I got a picture from him, and a letter. And it was a little letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got that-- that was somewhere in the beginning of '45. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] February or something like that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] March. And the-- and the war was over in May.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you got a letter, you must have been staying somewhere--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Rowne, finally, yeah. Oh yeah, that was in Rowne. I was already-- you know, that was-- the Russians were already there in that city. So I was-- I felt, like, free. And they bombed-- I, I stayed with a bunch of partisans in a house, where there was a shelter in a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they were doing some bombing-- Rowne was a pretty good-sized city, maybe about 100,000. And we were right near a bank. And they were bombarding the bank. Germans would bombard at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody was running to the basement, people from other houses. I was the only one in bed. And I thought, they're crazy, you know. I mean, they get killed-- they'll get killed there, too. But it didn't bother me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop the tape right here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. How about--
